title: Definitions
content: Key definitions: (a) 'authorized person' means authorized dealer, money changer, offshore banking unit, or person authorized under Section 10(1); (c) 'capital account transaction' means transaction which alters assets or liabilities outside India of persons resident in India or assets or liabilities in India of persons resident outside India; (d) 'currency' includes all currency notes, postal notes, postal orders, money orders, cheques, drafts, traveller's cheques, letters of credit, bills of exchange and promissory notes, credit cards or such other similar instruments; (j) 'current account transaction' means transaction other than capital account transaction including: payments due in connection with foreign trade, current business including services and short-term banking, remittances for living expenses, payments due as interest on loans and net income from investments, remittances for medical/studies/travel abroad; (q) 'person resident in India' means person residing in India for more than 182 days during the preceding financial year but does not include person who has gone out of India or who stays outside India for employment or carrying on business or vocation or for any other purpose indicating intention to stay outside India for uncertain period; (v) 'person resident outside India' means person who is not resident in India.

title: Dealing in Foreign Exchange
content: Save as otherwise provided in this Act, or with general or special permission of RBI: (a) no person shall deal in or transfer any foreign exchange or foreign security to any person not being an authorised person; (b) no person resident in India shall make any payment to or for the credit of any person resident outside India in any manner; (c) no person shall receive otherwise than through an authorised person, any payment by order or on behalf of any person resident outside India in any manner. Explanation: For purposes of this section, where any person in or resident in India receives any payment by order or on behalf of person resident outside India through any other person (including authorised person) without a corresponding inward remittance from outside India, such person shall be deemed to have received such payment otherwise than through an authorised person.

title: Holding of Foreign Exchange
content: Save as otherwise provided in this Act: (a) no person resident in India shall acquire, hold, own, possess or transfer any foreign exchange, foreign security or any immovable property situated outside India; (b) no person resident in India shall enter into or transfer any financial transaction in India with person resident outside India. Exemptions: Person returning from abroad may retain foreign currency up to limits specified. Authorized dealers may hold foreign exchange. Persons may hold authorized foreign exchange.

title: Current Account Transactions
content: Any person may sell or draw foreign exchange to or from an authorized person if such sale or drawing is a current account transaction. Central Government may, in public interest, in consultation with RBI, impose such reasonable restrictions for current account transactions as may be prescribed. Drawal of foreign exchange for the following purposes shall require prior approval of Central Government: (a) Cultural tours; (b) Remittances exceeding USD 250,000 per year (limit revised periodically); (c) Gift remittances exceeding limits; (d) Donations to foreign entities. No prior approval required for current account transactions like travel, medical treatment abroad, studies abroad within LRS limits.

title: Capital Account Transactions
content: (1) Subject to provisions of sub-section (2), any person may sell or draw foreign exchange to or from an authorized person for a capital account transaction. (2) RBI may, in consultation with Central Government, specify: (a) any class or classes of capital account transactions which are permissible; (b) the limit up to which foreign exchange shall be admissible for such transactions. (3) RBI shall not impose any restriction on drawal of foreign exchange for payments due on account of amortisation of loans or for depreciation of direct investments in ordinary course of business. (4) RBI may, by regulations, prohibit, restrict or regulate establishment in India of a branch, office or other place of business by a person resident outside India; acquisition or transfer of immovable property in India by person resident outside India; giving guarantee or surety; import and export of currency or currency notes; borrowing or lending in foreign exchange.

title: Export of Goods and Services
content: Every exporter of goods shall: (a) furnish to RBI or to authorised dealer a declaration in prescribed form with supporting documents containing true and correct material particulars including amount representing full export value; (b) furnish to RBI such other information as may be required; (c) where goods are exported to any place outside India by person other than exporter, name and address of that person shall be mentioned. Full export value shall be received within period prescribed by RBI. RBI may, in respect of goods or software exported, direct any amount representing sale proceeds or consideration for export of such goods/software shall be paid for in prescribed manner. Realization period for export proceeds is 9 months from date of export (extended from 9 to 15 months during COVID-19).

title: Realization and Repatriation of Foreign Exchange
content: Every person resident in India who has any amount of foreign exchange due or accrued in his favor or any person to whom foreign exchange has been paid shall take all reasonable steps to realize and repatriate such foreign exchange. The manner and period within which foreign exchange shall be repatriated shall be as prescribed by RBI. Person may hold in his own name any foreign exchange whose period of realization and repatriation has not expired. Non-resident may retain foreign exchange outside India even after becoming resident for period specified.

title: Penalties
content: (1) If any person contravenes any provision of this Act, or contravenes any rule, regulation, notification, direction or order issued in exercise of powers under this Act, or contravenes any condition subject to which an authorization is issued by RBI, he shall, upon adjudication, be liable to a penalty up to thrice the sum involved in such contravention where such amount is quantifiable, or up to two lakh rupees where the amount is not quantifiable, and where contravention is a continuing one, further penalty which may extend to five thousand rupees for every day after the first day during which the contravention continues. (1A) Where a contravention is committed by any person including a company referred to in clause (b) of section 1, if it is proved that such contravention took place with the consent or connivance of, or is attributable to any neglect on the part of any director, manager, secretary or other officer, such director, manager, secretary or other officer shall also be liable for penalty.

act: FEMA (Transfer or Issue of Security by a Person Resident Outside India) Regulations, 2017
title: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)
content: FDI permitted under automatic route up to 100% in most sectors including: Manufacturing, E-commerce (marketplace model), IT/BPO, Single brand retail trading, Wholesale/cash & carry, Hotels & Tourism, Construction development (conditions apply), Pharmaceuticals (brownfield 74%), Medical devices, Food processing. FDI requiring government approval: Multi-brand retail (51%), Print media (26%), Broadcasting (49-100% depending on category), Defence (over 74%), Banking private sector (74%), Insurance (74%), Pension (49%), Telecom (100% - subject to licensing), Satellites (74%), Mining (subject to sectoral laws). Prohibited sectors: Lottery, gambling, betting, chit funds, Nidhi company, real estate business (not construction), manufacturing cigars/cigarettes/tobacco, trading in TDRs, activities not open to private sector investment.

act: FEMA (Transfer or Issue of Security by a Person Resident Outside India) Regulations, 2017
title: Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI)
content: FPI allowed in: Listed equity shares, Government securities, Corporate bonds, Money market instruments, Derivatives, Investment by FPIs through Stock Exchanges (no prior approval). Investment limits: Single FPI - 10% of paid up capital of company; All FPIs combined - Sectoral cap for FDI (aggregate FPI + FDI must be within sectoral cap). Category of investors who can register as FPI: Sovereign wealth funds, Central banks, Pension funds, Mutual funds, Investment trusts, Insurance companies, Banks, Asset management companies, University funds, Endowments. KYC requirements apply. SEBI registration mandatory.

act: FEMA (Transfer or Issue of Security by a Person Resident Outside India) Regulations, 2017
title: Foreign Venture Capital Investment
content: SEBI registered Foreign Venture Capital Investor (FVCI) may invest: In equity or equity-linked instruments or debt instruments issued by Indian venture capital undertaking, or venture capital fund, or startup registered with DPIIT. Investment may be made in unlisted companies, subject to lock-in as applicable. Exit options: IPO, Secondary sale to other investors, Buy-back by company. Registration with SEBI mandatory. Eligible investor categories similar to FPI. Investment through primary market or secondary market permitted.

title: LRS for Resident Individuals
content: Resident individuals may remit up to USD 250,000 per financial year for any permissible current or capital account transaction or a combination of both. Permissible transactions: Private visits to any country; Gift or donation; Going abroad for employment; Emigration; Maintenance of close relatives abroad; Travel for business or attending conferences; Medical treatment abroad; Studies abroad; Purchase of immovable property abroad; Investment in equity and debt instruments abroad; Opening foreign currency account abroad; Setting up JV/WOS outside India. Prohibited: Remittance for margin money, margin calls, lottery tickets, banned magazines, purchase of FCCBs issued by Indian companies in overseas market. Not available to corporates, partnership firms, HUF, trusts. Form A2 declaration required.

title: External Commercial Borrowings
content: Eligible borrowers: Companies, LLPs, SEZ units, startups, REITs, InvITs. Eligible lenders: Multilateral institutions, Export credit agencies, Foreign equity holders, Banks, Financial institutions in FATF/IOSCO compliant jurisdictions. Forms: FCY denominated ECB, Rupee denominated ECB (Masala Bonds). Track I: Medium term (minimum average maturity 3 years); Track II: Long term (minimum average maturity 5 years); Track III: Rupee denominated. All-in-cost ceiling: Benchmark rate (SOFR, EURIBOR) plus 500 bps. End-use: General corporate purposes (for investment grade), Capital expenditure, Working capital, Repayment of rupee loans, On-lending by NBFCs. Prohibited end-uses: Real estate, equity investment, repayment of ECB availed for prohibited end-use. Reporting through ECB-2 form.

title: Overseas Direct Investment (ODI)
content: Who can invest: Indian Party (company incorporated in India, LLP, registered partnership firm, body corporate created under Act of Parliament/State Legislature, Resident Indian). Forms of investment: Equity in JV/WOS, Loan to JV/WOS, Guarantee on behalf of JV/WOS. Financial commitment limit: 400% of net worth as per last audited balance sheet (combined ceiling for all forms of FC). Automatic route: For activities not prohibited. Activities prohibited or requiring approval: Real estate (except permitted activities), Banking, Financial services without regulatory approval. Bona fide business test: Investment must be bona fide, for genuine business purpose, not round-tripping. Reporting through FC-TRS, APR forms. Pricing: At arm's length, valuation by Category I Merchant Banker for equity. Disinvestment proceeds to be repatriated within 90 days.
